inbound: 2acc0188-9422-4d05-92cf-4e692f69e2b0_Young-adults-into-fashion-designers.pdf\n\nAT A GLANCE When she vis- ited children’s home in Kenya Nur M’Nasria noted that much emphasis was placed on children as compaired to young adults. Young adults struggled to find fitting clothes as many donations comprised of chil- dren’s clothing. Maisha by Nur focusses on train- ing young-adults on fashion and design. Mentoring the next sustainable fashion designers in Kenya is still Nur’s vision that keeps her going PEOPLE DAILY / Monday, January 22, 202418 Take Off Guru Evans Conde, lead designer at Maisha by Nisria fashion and design studio INSET: Shanique Shinali, one of the beneficiaries. Nur M’Nasria founder of the design studio PD/HARRIET JAMES Nur M’Nasria-led fashion and design studio trains locals to produce authentic, handmade garments from ethically repurposed materials such as second hand clothes and textile wastes from companies Turning young adults into sustainable fashion designers Does #MeToo ring a bell? Through this hashtag, women from around the world amplified their voices on sexual harassment and sexual assault and continue to strongly condemn the vices. But sexual assault is not the only form of violence that women face. There has been a spike in cases of murder against Kenyan women who have been reportedly killed by men in private establishments under unclear circumstances. Gender based violence against women including physical abuse, human and organ trafficking, female genital mutilation and early marriages are widespread. These gross human rights violations against women have dire health and psychological consequences. It’s an impediment to peace, equality and development. Therefore, there is need to sensitise the public about the need to end violence against women. Violence against women hindrance to development QUICK FIGURES by Harriet James @PeopleDailyKe W hen Nur M’Nasria, a TV film producer came to Kenya in 2015 to film a documentary on slums in the country, she didn’t anticipate that the trip would change her life so much that she would make a difference in the life of others. The documentary focused on slums in India, Kenya and Egypt, but meeting abandoned children in Kenya moved her heart. “I was, especially touched by a children’s home that was surrounded by sewage in Kibera. I would watch the children tiptoe to get to the home and that depressed me,” recalls Nur who hails from Tunisia, but resides in Dubai . Ever since she was 15, Nur had been involved in humanitarian work, especially fundraising, mostly in The United Arab Emirates, but had never been involved in ground work. The Kenyan experience gave her the opportunity to do so. Struggling young adults She came back to the country in 2016 to start her first project, which entailed setting up playgrounds in underprivileged communities in Nairobi Baba Dogo and Ruiru. “We could make play materials from car tyres. I would also bring people to give us ideas on how to make play items from upcycled materials. I realised that I could do so much more,” continues Nur. The filmmaker discovered that much emphasis was being placed on children, while young adults were struggling because of lack of employment and direction in life. “I had visited so many children’s homes in Kenya and I realised that this category was not given the right attention and resources to assist them in their life, which always turned them into sex workers or thugs. Majority of the children’s homes were also businesses and the owners just wanted to get more funds— young adults are not attractive enough to bring donations. Additionally, once the children grow up, they leave the children’s home and most of them have nothing to do since they lack skills. I wanted to do vocational training and take in those young adults,” she explains. Nur further shares how when it came to clothing, the young adults would struggle to find a fit as more donations would often be children’s clothing— hence the young adults
